text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP VISIT — SURVEILLANCE / MAINTENANCE THERAPY COMPLETION NOTE

**PATIENT:** Margaret Osei-Bonsu
**AGE:** 49 years
**DIAGNOSIS:** High-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC)
**VISIT TYPE:** Scheduled oncology visit — planned end-of-maintenance assessment and niraparib cessation discussion
**TIMELINE:** Approximately 36 months from initial diagnosis; approximately 28 months on niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 49-year-old woman with a history of germline BRCA1-associated HGSC of the ovaries and fallopian tubes, now presenting for her scheduled oncology visit at the 36-month mark from her initial diagnosis. She is here to discuss completion of PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy and transition to routine surveillance. She has no new complaints.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, approximately 3 years ago. She initially presented with a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. Workup revealed bilateral ovarian masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis (PCI approximately 18 on preoperative imaging), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and markedly elevated CA-125 at 3,847 U/mL. Germline testing confirmed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (5382insC founder mutation). She subsequently underwent primary debulking surgery with exploratory laparotomy, TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants, and diaphragmatic stripping, achieving an R0 (no gross residual disease) resection. Surgical pathology confirmed stage pT3c pN1, with STIC of the fimbriated end of the right fallopian tube, four pelvic lymph nodes and one para-aortic lymph node positive, and multiple peritoneal implants cleared. She then received six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5-6 due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy). Her course was complicated by a Grade 2 carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 2, managed with desensitization protocol, and by Grade 2 anemia requiring transfusion after cycle 4. She completed all six cycles and achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response, with CA-125 normalizing to 11 U/mL post-chemotherapy and CT imaging showing no evidence of disease.

Given her germline BRCA1 status and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, she was initiated on niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy at 200 mg orally once daily, consistent with the SOLO-1 trial evidence base and FDA-approved indication. Approximately 3 weeks after starting niraparib, she developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (nadir platelets 58,000/uL), necessitating a treatment hold and subsequent dose reduction to 100 mg daily upon platelet recovery. At the reduced dose of 100 mg, she has experienced chronic low-grade thrombocytopenia — platelets have generally hovered in the 98,000–128,000/uL range (Grade 1-2) — which has been managed with close monitoring and without further dose reduction or interruption. She also developed niraparib-associated hypertension, managed with amlodipine, which has been successfully tapered now that she is approaching cessation of niraparib. She has otherwise tolerated niraparib 100 mg well.

Over the course of her maintenance therapy, serial CA-125 measurements have remained consistently within the normal range (range 7–11 U/mL). Surveillance CT scans at 12 months and 24 months post-chemotherapy completion demonstrated sustained no evidence of disease. She had an unrelated intercurrent issue of a 4 mm non-obstructing right renal calculus at approximately 30 months from diagnosis, which passed spontaneously on conservative management.

She has now completed approximately 28 months of niraparib maintenance therapy at the 100 mg dose. The original intent was to continue for 36 months per the SOLO-1 trial protocol, which demonstrated a sustained progression-free survival benefit in germline BRCA-mutated patients who achieved complete response. However, today's visit involves a shared decision-making discussion regarding the timing of maintenance therapy cessation. Per the updated PRIMA and SOLO-1 long-term data, and in the context of her excellent sustained remission, tolerability profile, and patient preference, as well as the cumulative hematologic and quality-of-life burden of long-term PARP inhibition, she and I have engaged in a detailed discussion about whether to continue to the full 36 months or to conclude therapy at this time point. After thorough counseling, the decision has been made to discontinue niraparib at 28 months of maintenance (approximately 36 months from diagnosis), representing a clinically reasonable course given the totality of evidence.

Today she has no new symptoms and feels well overall.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

General: No fatigue, no weight loss, no fevers, no night sweats. Energy level has improved substantially compared to during chemotherapy and early maintenance.
Neurological: Mild residual tingling in the bilateral toes, stable, non-progressive. This represents largely resolved Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy from prior paclitaxel; she notes this has improved markedly over the past 12 months.
Cardiovascular: No palpitations, no chest pain, no dyspnea. Blood pressure has been well-controlled on amlodipine with plans to taper.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea or vomiting. Appetite and bowel habits normal. No abdominal pain, distension, or bloating. No changes in bowel habits.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or urinary frequency beyond baseline (previously resolved renal calculus; no recurrent flank pain). No abnormal vaginal discharge or bleeding (post-hysterectomy).
Musculoskeletal: No new joint pain or myalgias. Reports occasional mild lower extremity stiffness attributed to deconditioning.
Hematologic: No unusual bruising, petechiae, gum bleeding, or other bleeding symptoms. Epistaxis absent.
Dermatologic: Hair regrowth complete; no new skin complaints.
Psychological/Psychiatric: Patient reports excellent psychological wellbeing. She has continued engagement with the oncology support group, which she finds meaningful. No depression or anxiety reported beyond occasional health-related worry, which she describes as manageable.
Endocrine: No vasomotor symptoms beyond those reported previously (surgical menopause managed without hormone replacement therapy given HGSC diagnosis). Some ongoing mild hot flashes but not disabling; she declines systemic HRT.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (as previously documented, updated)
1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, pT3c pN1 M0 — germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC — diagnosed at age 47; currently in sustained complete remission.
2. Surgical menopause following TAH-BSO at time of primary debulking surgery.
3. Niraparib-induced hypertension — on amlodipine (currently being tapered).
4. Paclitaxel-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy — largely resolved, Grade 0-1 residual.
5. Right lower pole nephrolithiasis, 4 mm, passed spontaneously (non-recurrent).
6. Carboplatin hypersensitivity (Grade 2, managed via desensitization; not relevant to current regimen).
7. Niraparib-induced thrombocytopenia — Grade 3 initially; dose-reduced, now chronic Grade 1-2 at 100 mg.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral lymph node dissection, peritoneal implant resection, right hemidiaphragm stripping) — R0 resection achieved.
- Diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy (prior to definitive surgery).

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer, diagnosed at age 38.
- Maternal grandmother: ovarian cancer, diagnosed at age 55.
- Both findings consistent with BRCA1 hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome.
- Patient's daughter (age 22): confirmed BRCA1 carrier on recent genetic testing; referred for risk-reduction counseling.
- Patient's sister (age 44): referred for genetic counseling.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a secondary school mathematics teacher who emigrated from Ghana to the United States at age 22. She is a lifelong nonsmoker and does not use alcohol. She has two adult children. She lives with a partner. She has resumed full-time work and has returned to recreational physical activities including hiking and yoga. She is engaged in the oncology psychosocial support program and describes strong social support.

ALLERGIES:
- Carboplatin — hypersensitivity reaction (Grade 2; managed via desensitization protocol during active treatment; not relevant to current or planned therapy).
- No other known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS (at this visit):
1. Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime — PARP inhibitor maintenance (being discontinued as of today)
2. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (tapered down from 10 mg; further taper planned after niraparib cessation, in coordination with PCP)
3. Duloxetine 30 mg orally once daily (for residual neuropathy — plan to continue for now, reassess at next visit)
4. Multivitamin once daily
5. Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed (PRN nausea — rarely used, likely to be discontinued)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8°C, Heart rate 72 bpm (regular), Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg (well-controlled; bilateral arm measurement consistent), Respiratory rate 14/min, O2 saturation 98% on room air, Weight 63.5 kg (stable, within 1 kg of baseline), Height 162 cm, BMI 24.2 kg/m2, ECOG performance status: 0.

General: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. She appears younger than her stated age. Affect is pleasant and engaged. Full scalp hair regrowth is noted.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Conjunctivae pink and well-perfused, no scleral icterus. Oropharynx moist and clear, no mucositis. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing, rales, or rhonchi. Good air movement.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable organomegaly. No palpable masses. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed, no evidence of hernia. No ascites appreciated on percussion or palpation. Bowel sounds normoactive.

Genitourinary / Pelvic: Post-hysterectomy/BSO. External genitalia normal. Vaginal vault intact and normal on inspection; no masses or abnormal discharge. Vault cuff without nodularity or induration to digital palpation. No adnexal fullness. Pelvic examination unremarkable.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Light touch sensation mildly diminished in the distal toes bilaterally, consistent with residual Grade 1 paclitaxel-induced sensory neuropathy. Proprioception intact. Gait steady and normal.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No petechiae or purpura. Skin intact.

Breast: Bilateral breast examination performed. No dominant masses, skin changes, nipple inversion, or discharge. No axillary adenopathy. (Note: recent bilateral breast MRI at outside institution — no suspicious findings; report on file.)

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Complete Blood Count (obtained today):
- WBC: 5.4 x10^9/L (normal)
- Absolute Neutrophil Count: 3.2 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 12.3 g/dL (normal for age; improved from nadir during chemotherapy and early maintenance)
- Hematocrit: 37.1%
- MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)
- Platelets: 128,000/uL (mild thrombocytopenia, Grade 1 by CTCAE; trending upward compared to prior nadir of 58,000/uL; has been stable at 98,000–128,000/uL range over past several months on 100 mg dosing)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride: 103 mEq/L, CO2: 26 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.78 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- Glucose: 88 mg/dL
- Calcium: 9.3 mg/dL
- AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 19 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- Total protein: 7.1 g/dL, Albumin: 4.2 g/dL
- LDH: 148 U/L (normal)

Tumor Markers:
- CA-125: 9 U/mL (within normal limits, reference <35 U/mL; has been consistently normal for approximately 27 months — range 7–11 U/mL across all surveillance measurements since post-chemotherapy normalization)
- HE4: not redrawn today (last value within normal limits)

No imaging performed at this visit. Next CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned in approximately 3 months per revised surveillance schedule.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 49-year-old woman with germline BRCA1-associated (5382insC) high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, who achieved an R0 resection at primary debulking surgery and a complete biochemical and radiographic response to six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel. She has now been on niraparib maintenance therapy for approximately 28 months (at the dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily following early Grade 3 thrombocytopenia). She continues to be in sustained complete remission with CA-125 consistently at 9 U/mL, normal biochemical parameters, and excellent performance status.

1. PARP INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE THERAPY — DISCONTINUATION:
We had a lengthy and substantive shared decision-making discussion today regarding the timing of niraparib cessation. Per the SOLO-1 trial (Moore et al., NEJM 2018 and updated long-term analysis), olaparib maintenance for 2 years in germline BRCA-mutated patients with complete or partial response to first-line platinum demonstrated a landmark improvement in progression-free survival, with benefit extending well beyond the treatment period. The PRIMA trial provides supporting evidence for niraparib. The originally planned duration was 3 years (36 months from initiation). She has completed 28 months of niraparib, and she has been on PARP inhibition as a class for this duration.

We discussed the following in our shared decision-making conversation:
- The evidence base supporting 2–3 years of PARP inhibitor maintenance in this setting.
- Her cumulative toxicity burden: chronic Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia throughout maintenance, hypertension requiring pharmacologic management (now well-controlled), and the psychosocial burden of ongoing medication and monitoring.
- Her sustained and durable complete remission — 28+ months of surveillance with no biochemical or radiographic evidence of recurrence.
- The fact that most of the progression-free survival benefit of PARP inhibitor maintenance appears to accrue within the first 24 months of maintenance, with diminishing marginal benefit thereafter in the maintained-remission population.
- Patient preference: Ms. Osei-Bonsu has thoughtfully considered this decision and expresses a clear preference to discontinue niraparib at this juncture, accepting the theoretical residual risk of recurrence in exchange for freedom from ongoing hematologic monitoring, antihypertensive medication, and the daily medication burden.
- The option to restart PARP inhibitor or pursue other therapy at the time of recurrence, should recurrence ever occur, was discussed.

Decision: Niraparib (Zejula) is discontinued effective today. She was counseled that platelets are expected to recover over the coming weeks to months following cessation, as we have observed with prior holds. She was advised to watch for any symptoms that might suggest thrombocytopenia (unusual bruising, petechiae, prolonged bleeding) in the interim before platelet recovery, though at her current platelet level this is low-risk.

2. HYPERTENSION — AMLODIPINE TAPER:
Blood pressure today is 122/76 mmHg, well-controlled. Given niraparib cessation, the contribution of PARP inhibitor to her hypertension will resolve. Plan to taper amlodipine from 5 mg to 2.5 mg in 4 weeks, then reassess for discontinuation. Coordinating with her primary care physician. She is instructed to monitor blood pressure at home biweekly.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY:
Residual Grade 0-1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in bilateral toes, largely resolved from its peak during and after chemotherapy. This is stable and non-limiting. Duloxetine 30 mg daily will be continued for now; reassess at next visit with consideration for taper given the marked improvement.

4. SURVEILLANCE PLAN (post-maintenance):
Now that niraparib is discontinued, she will transition into an active surveillance protocol consistent with current NCCN and SGO guidelines for HGSC in sustained remission after PARP inhibitor maintenance:
- CA-125 every 3 months for the next 2 years, then every 6 months thereafter.
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 6 months for the next 2 years, then annually thereafter (or sooner if symptoms or rising CA-125).
- Surveillance visits: every 3 months for the next 1 year, then every 6 months.
- Patient educated on symptoms of recurrence: new or worsening abdominal pain or bloating, increased abdominal girth, early satiety, change in bowel or bladder habits, unintentional weight loss. She is instructed to contact our office promptly for any new or concerning symptoms rather than waiting for a scheduled appointment.
- Next appointment: 3 months (with CA-125) and a CT scan around that timeframe.

5. BREAST CANCER SURVEILLANCE (germline BRCA1):
Given her confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, she remains at significantly elevated lifetime risk for breast cancer. Current breast surveillance:
- Annual bilateral breast MRI alternating with mammography every 6 months per NCCN guidelines for BRCA1 carriers.
- Most recent bilateral breast MRI (outside institution): no suspicious findings on file.
- Risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy (RRM) was discussed again today. She declines RRM at this time but understands the risk-reduction benefit and remains open to revisiting the discussion. She is to continue intensive surveillance.
- Referral for follow-up with hereditary cancer genetics program to ensure she is enrolled in the appropriate breast surveillance program and to reassess risk-reduction options annually.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND QUALITY OF LIFE:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has demonstrated remarkable resilience throughout this journey. She has returned to full-time teaching, is engaged in yoga and hiking, and is actively involved in the oncology support group. She describes her overall wellbeing as excellent. We discussed the potential psychological impact of transitioning off active treatment — the so-called "Damocles syndrome" — and the anxiety that some patients experience when they no longer feel "protected" by ongoing therapy. She acknowledges some background health anxiety but feels well-equipped to manage it. Referral to the survivorship clinic was offered; she is interested and will be scheduled accordingly.

7. FAMILY GENETIC IMPLICATIONS:
Patient's daughter (age 22) has tested positive for BRCA1 and is engaged with genetic counseling and risk-reduction planning — appropriate. Patient's sister (age 44) has been referred; encouraged to follow up on the status of her sister's evaluation at next visit. Patient remains a strong advocate for familial testing within her extended family network.

8. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS:
Ongoing mild vasomotor symptoms (surgical menopause). Patient has declined systemic HRT; discussed low-dose topical vaginal estrogen for any urogenital atrophy symptoms — she is interested in this option and was provided with a prescription for low-dose vaginal estradiol (0.01% cream), which carries minimal systemic absorption and would not be contraindicated in this setting; referral to menopause specialist offered if desired.

---

SUMMARY:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu has completed an approximately 28-month course of niraparib maintenance therapy and is in sustained complete remission at 36 months from her initial diagnosis of FIGO stage IIIC, germline BRCA1-associated high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. Her prognosis at this juncture is encouraging: R0 resection, complete biochemical and radiographic response, BRCA1-positivity (highest benefit subgroup from PARP inhibitor maintenance), and durable remission beyond 2.5 years. Niraparib is discontinued today per shared decision-making. She transitions into surveillance. I am proud of the work she has done to get to this point and reassured by her excellent status today.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes, including counseling, examination, and care coordination.

Electronically signed,
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist / Medical Oncologist
Gynecologic Oncology and Women's Cancer Program
